blocks.statement: I can describe Faraday's discovery (moving a magnet near a coil induces a voltage and current), predict the direction of induced current using flux change, and explain why generators and transformers all run on this principle.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Day 13: current creates B. Today: the reverse — changing B creates current.
blocks.connection: Together, Days 13 and 14 are the ENGINE of every motor, generator, and transformer. Day 15 puts both into one machine.

blocks.markdown:
## Faraday flips Oersted's arrow

Oersted (1820): current → magnetic field. Michael Faraday spent a decade hunting the reverse, and in 1831 found it — with a catch that IS the whole law:

**A CHANGING magnetic flux through a coil induces a voltage (EMF) in the coil.**

Push a magnet INTO a coil → the ammeter swings one way. Pull it OUT → it swings the other way. Hold it perfectly STILL — even a huge magnet — **nothing**. It's the CHANGE that matters, never the value.

**Magnetic flux Φ** measures how much B-field passes through the loop: Φ = B · A · cos θ (roughly), in webers (Wb). Flux changes when ANY of B, A, or the angle changes — move the magnet, or spin the loop.

The size of the induced voltage: **ε ≈ N · ΔΦ/Δt**. Faster change → bigger voltage. More turns N → bigger voltage.

This is how a **generator** works: spin a coil inside a magnetic field, the flux through it changes every instant of the rotation, and voltage is induced. Wind turbines, hydroelectric dams, car alternators, hand-crank flashlights — all the same trick: mechanical motion in, electricity out. The exact opposite of a motor — and Day 15 shows they're literally the same machine.

terms.definition: A CHANGING magnetic flux through a coil INDUCES a voltage (EMF) in the coil. The faster the change, or the more turns of wire, the bigger the induced voltage. Example: push a magnet INTO a coil → ammeter swings one way. Pull it OUT → swings the other way. STILL magnet → no current.
terms.cognate: Sp. ley de Faraday · Pt. lei de Faraday · HC lwa Faraday

terms.definition: The amount of magnetic field passing through a loop. Φ = B · A · cos θ (roughly). Units: webers (Wb) = T·m². Example: a 0.1 T field through a 0.01 m² loop perpendicular to the field: Φ = 0.001 Wb. Rotate the loop 90° so field is parallel → Φ drops to 0. Flux changes when ANY of B, A, or angle changes.
terms.cognate: Sp. flujo magnético · Pt. fluxo magnético · HC fliks mayetik

terms.definition: A machine that converts mechanical motion into electrical voltage by spinning a coil inside a magnetic field. The OPPOSITE of a motor. Example: wind turbines, hydroelectric dams, car alternators, hand-crank flashlights — all generators. Each rotation changes flux → induced voltage → current.
terms.cognate: Sp. generador · Pt. gerador · HC jeneratè

blocks.title: It's the CHANGE that matters
blocks.markdown: It's the CHANGE in B that matters, not the value. A constant B-field (even huge) produces NO induced voltage. A magnet sitting motionless inside a coil does nothing at all.

blocks.instruction: Coil of wire connected to an ammeter. A bar magnet is being PUSHED into the coil. Show the ammeter needle deflecting. Then a second sketch: pulling out → needle deflects the OTHER way.
prompts: Sketch 1: magnet pushed IN, ammeter needle deflecting.
Sketch 2: magnet pulled OUT, needle deflecting the OTHER way.

blocks.prompt: INDUCED VOLTAGE FROM CHANGING FLUX — A 100-turn coil has a magnetic flux that changes by Φ = 0.005 Wb over Δt = 0.1 s. What's the induced EMF? (ε ≈ N · ΔΦ/Δt.)
blocks.given: N = 100 turns · ΔΦ = 0.005 Wb · Δt = 0.1 s
blocks.answer: ε = 5 V induced across the coil's ends — drives current if connected to a circuit.

blocks.prompt: FASTER MOTION — Same 100-turn coil, same ΔΦ = 0.005 Wb, but you push the magnet TWICE as fast (Δt = 0.05 s). What's the new EMF?
blocks.givenHint: N = 100 turns · ΔΦ = 0.005 Wb · Δt = 0.05 s.
blocks.equationHint: Same equation as the worked example — halving Δt should do something predictable to ε.

blocks.title: ★ Why grid power is AC
blocks.markdown: Generators produce AC (alternating current) because the rotating coil's flux changes sinusoidally — sometimes increasing, sometimes decreasing. The induced voltage alternates direction with each half-rotation. That's why grid power is AC.

blocks.prompt: Why does pushing a magnet into a coil light up an attached LED — but holding it still does nothing? Answer in two sentences using the word 'changing'.
blocks.frame: Faraday's law says a ___ magnetic flux through a coil induces a ___. The induced voltage is BIGGER when (a) the change is ___, (b) there are ___ turns of wire. A generator works by ___ a coil through a magnetic field.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **37.1–37.2 and 37.7–37.8** (electromagnetic induction, Faraday's law, induced fields, and electromagnetic waves). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
